name: Login, Session & OAuth2/JWT
description: How modern web authentication works: cookie attributes, session vs token, JWT structure, the OAuth2 authorization-code flow, token refresh, keeping sessions alive, and the security checklist. From the login tutorial.

topics.name: Cookie Attributes
topics.description: HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite, Path/Domain, expiry, and the __Secure-/__Host- prefixes — what each one defends against.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Cookies are small pieces of data the server asks the browser to store and send back with future requests — this is how a login session usually works. Each attribute on a cookie is a switch that controls where and when the browser will send it.

## What problem does it solve?

A session cookie is effectively a key to the user's account. Each attribute closes one specific way of stealing or misusing that key.

## How it works

- HttpOnly — JavaScript on the page cannot read the cookie (document.cookie returns nothing). So even if an attacker manages to inject a script into your page (XSS), the script cannot steal the session.
- Secure — the cookie is only sent over HTTPS, never over plain HTTP. Protects it from being read on the network (public wifi).
- SameSite — controls whether the cookie is sent when the request comes FROM ANOTHER SITE. Strict: never. Lax (the default): only on normal link clicks, not on hidden form posts. None: always — allowed only together with Secure. This is the defense against CSRF (another site triggering requests with your cookie).
- Path and Domain — limit which URLs and (sub)domains receive the cookie.
- Expires / Max-Age — when the cookie dies. With neither set, it lives until the browser closes.
- __Host- name prefix — the browser only accepts the cookie if it is Secure, Path=/, and has no Domain set. It is the "maximum strictness" marker.

A well-protected session cookie looks like: Set-Cookie: __Host-session=abc; Secure; Path=/; HttpOnly; SameSite=Strict.

## Key terms

- XSS — injected script; HttpOnly stops it from reading cookies
- CSRF — forged cross-site request; SameSite stops the cookie from riding along
- Session cookie — no expiry set, dies with the browser
- __Host- prefix — enforced strict settings, cannot be scoped wider

topics.name: Session vs Token Authentication
topics.description: Server-side sessions, stateless JWTs, and third-party OAuth2 — trade-offs in scaling, logout, and revocation.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The two main ways a server can remember who you are after login: server-side sessions and tokens (JWT). A third option, OAuth2, lets another company (Google, GitHub) handle the login itself.

## What problem does it solve?

HTTP forgets everything between requests. After login, every request must somehow prove "this is still Anna". The question is WHERE that proof lives.

## How it works

Server-side session: after login, the server creates a record in its own storage (memory or Redis) — "session abc123 = user 42" — and gives the browser only the ID in a cookie. Every request sends the ID; the server looks it up.
- Good: logout is instant (delete the record); easy to revoke.
- Bad: the server must store every session, and with multiple servers they all need access to the same session store.

Token (JWT): after login, the server signs a token that CONTAINS the user data (id, role, expiry) and stores nothing. The browser sends the token with each request; the server just checks the signature.
- Good: no server storage, any server can verify — easy to scale; works well for mobile and cross-domain.
- Bad: logout is hard. The token stays valid until it expires — the server cannot "delete" it without keeping a blocklist, which brings back server state. A stolen token works until expiry.

OAuth2 (third-party login): the user proves who they are at Google; your backend receives a code, exchanges it for tokens, and then typically issues its own session or JWT.

## Key terms

- Session store — server-side memory/Redis holding session records
- Stateless — the server keeps nothing; everything needed is in the token
- Revocation — invalidating access before expiry; easy for sessions, hard for JWTs
- Blocklist / blacklist — the workaround that makes JWT logout possible

topics.name: JWT Structure & Storage
topics.description: header.payload.signature, standard vs custom claims, what the signature guarantees, and where to store the token on the client.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

A JWT (JSON Web Token) is a string with three parts separated by dots: header.payload.signature. The header says which algorithm signed it, the payload carries the data ("claims": user id, role, expiry), and the signature proves nobody changed it.

## What problem does it solve?

The server wants to hand the client a piece of data ("you are user 42 with role admin, valid until 15:00") and later trust that data WITHOUT storing anything. The signature makes that possible: the data travels with the client, but any tampering is detectable.

## How it works

The header and payload are just Base64-encoded JSON — encoded, NOT encrypted. Anyone can decode and read them. Never put secrets (passwords, private data) in a JWT payload.

The signature is computed from the first two parts plus a secret key that only the server knows. If anyone edits a claim (say, changes role to admin), the signature no longer matches and the server rejects the token. That is the whole guarantee: not secrecy, but integrity.

The client MAY decode the payload — for example to read the expiry time and refresh in advance. But only the server can VERIFY the token, because verification needs the secret key.

Standard claims worth knowing: exp (expires at), iat (issued at), jti (a unique token id, useful for revocation).

## Where to store it in the browser

- localStorage: survives reloads, but any injected script (XSS) can read it — the classic way tokens get stolen.
- HttpOnly cookie: JavaScript cannot touch it, so XSS cannot steal it; you must think about CSRF instead.
- In memory only: safest, but a page refresh logs the user out unless there is a refresh mechanism.

Common recommendation: HttpOnly cookie, or short-lived tokens + refresh tokens if you must use localStorage.

## Key terms

- Claims — the key–value data inside the payload (user_id, role, exp)
- Base64 — encoding, readable by anyone; not encryption
- Signature — tamper-proof seal made with the server's secret key
- exp / iat / jti — expiry, issue time, token id

topics.name: OAuth2 Authorization Code Flow
topics.description: The full third-party login flow step by step, why the client secret never reaches the browser, and how OAuth2 relates to JWT.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The OAuth2 authorization code flow is the standard mechanism behind every "Log in with Google" button. It lets your app confirm a user's identity through a provider (Google, GitHub) without ever seeing their password.

## What problem does it solve?

Users do not want another password; you do not want to store passwords. But letting a third party do the login safely is tricky — the flow is designed so that the sensitive pieces never pass through the browser, where they could be stolen.

## How it works

- 1. The user clicks "Login with Google". Your frontend redirects them to Google, sending along your app's client_id, a redirect_uri (where to come back), the scopes you want (profile, email), and a random state string.
- 2. The user logs in at Google and approves.
- 3. Google redirects back to your site with a short-lived, single-use authorization code (and echoes the state back — your backend checks it matches, which blocks forged callbacks).
- 4. Your BACKEND sends the code + client_id + client_secret to Google's token endpoint. The client_secret lives only on your server — this is the crucial rule. Anything in the browser can be read by the user or an attacker; the code alone is useless without the secret.
- 5. Google returns an ID token (who the user is) and an access token.
- 6. Your backend reads the identity, creates or finds the user in its own database, and issues its OWN session or JWT. From now on the app uses its own token — Google is out of the picture.

One clarification interviewers love: OAuth2 is a PROTOCOL, JWT is a TOKEN FORMAT. They are different things that often appear together. OAuth2 providers may hand out JWTs or opaque random strings.

## Key terms

- client_id / client_secret — your app's public name and its server-only password
- Authorization code — single-use voucher, exchanged server-side for tokens
- state — random value that must come back unchanged; blocks CSRF on the callback
- Redirect URI whitelist — provider only redirects to pre-registered URLs
- ID token vs access token — who the user is vs what may be accessed

topics.name: Token Refresh & Session Keep-Alive
topics.description: Short-lived access token + long-lived refresh token, rotation, device tracking, and the three keep-alive strategies.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The two-token system used by most modern apps: a short-lived access token (15–30 minutes) used on every API request, and a long-lived refresh token (days or weeks) whose only job is to get new access tokens.

## What problem does it solve?

A conflict: tokens should be short-lived (if one is stolen, the damage window is small), but users should stay logged in for weeks without retyping their password. Splitting the job into two tokens solves both.

## How it works

The normal cycle:
- 1. The access token expires; an API call returns 401 Unauthorized.
- 2. The client calls the refresh endpoint. The refresh token rides along automatically because it is stored in an HttpOnly cookie (JavaScript cannot read it).
- 3. The server checks the refresh token against its own records — is it active, unexpired, from the same device? — and issues a fresh access token.
- 4. The client retries the original request. The user notices nothing. In practice this is one HTTP interceptor: catch 401, refresh once, replay.

Protecting the refresh token, since it is the powerful one:
- Store it hashed in the database, with device info and expiry — this makes real logout and "log out everywhere" possible.
- Rotation: each refresh issues a NEW refresh token and kills the old one. If a stolen copy is used, it races the real one and gets detected.
- Logout must invalidate the refresh token on the SERVER. Deleting a token from localStorage alone does nothing — a stolen copy would still work.

## Keeping the session alive while the user is idle

- Silent refresh: refresh on a timer (e.g. every 10 minutes).
- Activity-based: on click/keypress, check the token's expiry and refresh if it is close.
- Sliding window: every refresh restarts the full refresh-token lifetime, so active users stay in forever and inactive ones age out.

## Key terms

- Access token — short-lived, sent with every request
- Refresh token — long-lived, HttpOnly cookie, only for renewal
- Token rotation — new refresh token on every use; detects theft
- 401 → refresh → retry — the interceptor pattern

topics.name: Auth Security Best Practices
topics.description: The login-system checklist — HTTPS, hashing, rate limiting, CSRF defenses — and the classic mistakes.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The checklist you walk through before a login system goes live — each item is a real attack it prevents.

## What problem does it solve?

Login systems fail in well-known, repeated ways. This list is the accumulated "we learned it the hard way" of the industry.

## How it works

The must-haves, each with its reason:
- HTTPS everywhere — a token is a password; on plain HTTP it can be read off the network.
- HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite on auth cookies — blocks script access (XSS) and cross-site sending (CSRF).
- Password hashing with bcrypt or argon2 — these are deliberately slow, so stolen hashes cannot be brute-forced quickly. MD5/SHA1 are fast and therefore unsafe for passwords.
- Rate limiting on login — e.g. 5 attempts per 15 minutes, against automated password guessing.
- Token expiry — access tokens 15–60 minutes, refresh tokens bounded too.
- Signature verification on EVERY request — a token you do not verify is just a suggestion.
- Server-side logout — invalidate the session/refresh token in the database, not only in the browser.
- CORS limited to your own origins.

## CSRF defenses, by auth style

- Cookie session → SameSite=Strict on the cookie.
- JWT in a header → an X-CSRF-Token custom header (other sites cannot set custom headers on requests to you).
- OAuth2 → the state parameter, verified on the callback.

## The classic mistakes to catch in review

JWT in localStorage on a site with any XSS risk; logout that only clears the browser; refresh tokens that never expire; the JWT secret reachable from client code; accepting tokens without verifying signatures.

## Key terms

- bcrypt / argon2 — slow password hashes, the only acceptable kind
- Rate limiting — capped login attempts against brute force
- Credential stuffing — trying leaked passwords in bulk; rate limits + hashing blunt it
- Server-side invalidation — logout that actually revokes

questions.title: Session vs JWT
questions.prompt: What is the difference between server-side sessions and JWT-based authentication? When would you pick each?
questions.answerStructureHint: Where the state lives → scaling implications → logout/revocation difference → when each fits.

expectedPoints.label: Where the state lives
expectedPoints.description: Session: server stores the record (memory/Redis), cookie carries only an ID. JWT: all data in the token, server stateless.

expectedPoints.label: Scaling trade-off
expectedPoints.description: Sessions need sync or a shared store across servers; JWTs verify anywhere.

expectedPoints.label: Logout / revocation difference
expectedPoints.description: Session logout is instant (delete the record); JWT stays valid until expiry unless blacklisted.

followUps.prompt: A user's JWT is stolen. What can you actually do about it?

expectedPoints.label: Mitigating a stolen JWT
expectedPoints.description: Short expiry limits the damage; jti + blacklist or rotating the signing key revokes at a cost.

questions.title: Securing a session cookie
questions.prompt: You are setting the session cookie for a login system. Which cookie attributes do you set, and what does each one defend against?
questions.answerStructureHint: Attribute by attribute: flag → attack it prevents. Finish with the __Host- prefix as the strict default.

expectedPoints.label: HttpOnly against XSS
expectedPoints.description: Not readable from document.cookie, so injected script cannot steal it.

expectedPoints.label: Secure = HTTPS only
expectedPoints.description: Never sent over plain HTTP; defends against wifi sniffing.

expectedPoints.label: SameSite against CSRF
expectedPoints.description: Strict = same-site only; Lax allows top-level GET navigation; None requires Secure.

expectedPoints.label: Scope and expiry attributes
expectedPoints.description: Path/Domain scoping, Expires/Max-Age lifetime, __Secure-/__Host- prefixes for enforced strictness.

questions.title: What is inside a JWT
questions.prompt: Walk me through the structure of a JWT. What can the client safely do with it, and what must only happen on the server?

expectedPoints.label: Header.payload.signature
expectedPoints.description: Three dot-separated Base64 segments: algorithm, claims, signature.

expectedPoints.label: Readable, not encrypted
expectedPoints.description: Base64 is encoding, not encryption — anyone can read the payload; never put secrets in it.

expectedPoints.label: Signature = integrity guarantee
expectedPoints.description: Only the server knows the secret; altering a claim invalidates the signature — verification is server-only.

expectedPoints.label: Standard claims
expectedPoints.description: iat/exp/nbf/jti from RFC 7519; jti enables revocation.

followUps.prompt: Where do you store the JWT on the client, and what is the trade-off of each option?

expectedPoints.label: localStorage vs HttpOnly cookie vs memory
expectedPoints.description: localStorage is XSS-exposed; HttpOnly cookie is JS-invisible but needs CSRF care; memory dies on refresh.

questions.title: OAuth2 authorization code flow
questions.prompt: Explain the OAuth2 authorization code flow end to end — "Login with Google". Why does the browser never receive the client secret?
questions.answerStructureHint: Redirect with client_id+state → user consents → code to callback → backend exchanges code+secret for tokens → app issues its own token.

expectedPoints.label: Redirect with client_id, redirect_uri, scope, state
expectedPoints.description: The frontend sends the user to the provider's authorize endpoint.

expectedPoints.label: Backend exchanges the code with the secret
expectedPoints.description: The authorization code returns via the callback; only the backend trades code + client_secret for tokens.
acceptedSignals: client secret stays on the server

expectedPoints.label: Why the secret never reaches the browser
expectedPoints.description: Anything in the browser is readable; the code alone is useless without the secret, and it is single-use.
acceptedSignals: code is useless without the secret

expectedPoints.label: App issues its own token afterwards
expectedPoints.description: Backend reads identity from the ID token, creates the user, and issues its own JWT/session — Google is out of the loop.

followUps.prompt: Is OAuth2 the same thing as JWT?

expectedPoints.label: Protocol vs token format
expectedPoints.description: OAuth2 is an authorization protocol; JWT is a token format. OAuth2 may use JWTs (ID tokens) or opaque tokens.

questions.title: Why refresh tokens exist
questions.prompt: Why do we split authentication into an access token and a refresh token? Walk through what happens when the access token expires mid-session.

expectedPoints.label: Short access + long refresh
expectedPoints.description: Access: 15–30 min, used on every request — stolen means limited damage. Refresh: days/weeks, only for renewal.

expectedPoints.label: 401 → refresh → retry
expectedPoints.description: Expired access token returns 401; client calls the refresh endpoint (HttpOnly cookie rides along), gets a new access token, replays the request — invisible to the user.

expectedPoints.label: Refresh token protection
expectedPoints.description: HttpOnly cookie only, hashed server-side tracking, rotation on use, device fingerprint.

followUps.prompt: How do you keep the session alive while the user is active but idle between requests?

expectedPoints.label: Keep-alive strategies
expectedPoints.description: Silent refresh on a timer, activity-based refresh near expiry, or a sliding window extending on each refresh.

questions.title: CSRF defenses in modern login
questions.prompt: How do you defend against CSRF, and how does the defense differ between cookie-based sessions, JWT-in-header, and OAuth2?

expectedPoints.label: Understands the attack
expectedPoints.description: A malicious site triggers requests to your API riding on the browser's automatically-attached cookies.

expectedPoints.label: Sessions: SameSite cookie
expectedPoints.description: SameSite=Strict stops the cookie from accompanying cross-site requests.

expectedPoints.label: Headers and state parameter
expectedPoints.description: JWT: X-CSRF-Token custom header (cross-origin pages cannot set custom headers). OAuth2: random state verified at the callback.

questions.title: Login system security checklist
questions.prompt: You are reviewing a login implementation before it ships. What is on your security checklist, and which classic mistakes do you look for?
questions.answerStructureHint: Transport → storage → credentials → abuse limits → lifecycle (expiry, logout). Then the classic mistakes.

expectedPoints.label: HTTPS + hardened cookies
expectedPoints.description: HTTPS mandatory; HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite on auth cookies; CORS restricted to trusted origins.

expectedPoints.label: Password hashing + rate limiting
expectedPoints.description: bcrypt/argon2 (never MD5/SHA1); login attempts rate-limited against credential stuffing.

expectedPoints.label: Token lifecycle discipline
expectedPoints.description: Expiries set (access short, refresh bounded), signatures verified on every request, logout invalidates server-side.

expectedPoints.label: Spots the classic mistakes
expectedPoints.description: JWT in localStorage, client-only logout, never-expiring refresh tokens, secret reachable from the client.
